# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# TAGS: labs, interpretation, reference ranges, functional ranges, optimal ranges, blood tests, CBC, CMP, metabolic panel, lipid panel, thyroid panel, hormone panel, inflammation markers, hs-CRP, ESR, ferritin, iron panel, vitamin D, B12, folate, homocysteine, HbA1c, fasting insulin, liver enzymes, AST, ALT, GGT, kidney function, BUN, creatinine, eGFR, urinalysis, complete blood count, what do my labs mean, results explanation, abnormal result, out of range, critical value, high value, low value, unexpected finding, lab anomaly, interpret my results, what does this mean, flag, concerning result, lab interpretation, blood test, results, lab values, reference range, optimal range, functional range, AST ALT, iron studies, vitamin D level, B12 level, hormone levels, inflammatory markers, CRP, fasting glucose, insulin, understanding labs, what do my results mean, free testosterone assay, calculated free testosterone, direct free testosterone, equilibrium dialysis, Vermeulen equation, percent free testosterone, cortisol single snapshot, cortisol AM limitation, DUTCH test, salivary cortisol, assay method, calculated ratios, derived indices, TyG index, triglyceride glucose index, NLR, neutrophil lymphocyte ratio, PLR, platelet lymphocyte ratio, AST ALT ratio, calcium phosphorus ratio, sodium potassium ratio, osmolality index, TC HDL ratio, LDL HDL ratio, cortisol DHEA ratio, testosterone estradiol ratio, albumin globulin ratio, atherogenic index of plasma, AIP, eAG, estimated average glucose, HOMA-IR calculation, ratio interpretation, index calculation, derived biomarker, composite marker, cross-system ratio, inflammation index, metabolic index, cardiovascular ratio, hormonal ratio, stress ratio, hydration index, mineral balance ratio, MPV, mean platelet volume, RDW, UIBC, TIBC, transferrin saturation, C-peptide, uric acid, bilirubin, chloride, phosphorus, osmolality, CPK, creatine kinase, Lp(a), lipoprotein a, ApoB, apolipoprotein B
# CATEGORY: Clinical Tools
# USE FOR: Interpreting lab results, understanding blood tests, optimal vs reference ranges, what lab values mean
# COUPLED_SYSTEMS: 36_TESTING_GUIDE, 53_QUICK_REFERENCE_CARDS, 50_DIAGNOSTIC_DECISION_TREES, 06_METABOLIC, 09_HPA_ADRENAL, 11_IMMUNE, 13_DETOX, 17_MALE_HORMONES, 18_FEMALE_HORMONES, 19_CARDIOVASCULAR, 20_JOINTS_MSK, 39_HYDRATION_ELECTROLYTES, 61_TERRAIN_ASSESSMENT, 62_CLINICAL_REASONING_ANOMALIES, 64_DOSSIER_PROTOCOL
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# LAB TEST INTERPRETATION GUIDE
# ══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

# ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐

 === SECTION 1: CLAIMS DISCIPLINE [INTERNAL_ONLY] ===
## UNDERSTANDING LAB RESULTS

### "Normal" vs. Optimal

**Lab reference ranges are based on population statistics** — typically the 
middle 95% of people tested.

**Problems with this:**
- Includes sick people
- Includes people on their way to disease
- Very wide ranges
- "Normal" doesn't mean optimal

**Example: TSH**
- Lab "normal": 0.4-4.5 mIU/L
- Many people with TSH of 3.5 have hypothyroid symptoms
- Optimal range: 0.5-2.0 mIU/L

**We use functional/optimal ranges** — where you function best and disease 
risk is lowest.

---

## COMPREHENSIVE METABOLIC PANEL

### Glucose

| Range | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| <70 | Hypoglycemia |
| 70-85 | Optimal |
| 86-99 | "Normal" but trending |
| 100-125 | Prediabetes |
| ≥126 | Diabetes |

### Hemoglobin A1c

| Range | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| <5.0% | Optimal |
| 5.0-5.4% | Good |
| 5.5-5.6% | Elevated, early dysfunction |
| 5.7-6.4% | Prediabetes |
| ≥6.5% | Diabetes |

### Fasting Insulin

**Often not ordered but CRITICAL for metabolic health.**

| Range | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| <5 µIU/mL | Optimal |
| 5-7 | Acceptable |
| 8-10 | Insulin resistance developing |
| >10 | Significant insulin resistance |
| >15 | Advanced insulin resistance |

### HOMA-IR (Calculated)

**Insulin Resistance Index: (Fasting Glucose × Fasting Insulin) ÷ 405**

| Range | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| <1.0 | Optimal |
| 1.0-2.0 | Acceptable |
| 2.0-3.0 | Early insulin resistance |
| >3.0 | Significant insulin resistance |

### Liver Enzymes

**AST (SGOT) and ALT (SGPT)**

| Range | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| <20 | Optimal |
| 20-35 | Normal |
| 36-50 | Mildly elevated (investigate) |
| >50 | Significantly elevated |

**ALT > AST:** Often fatty liver, metabolic
**AST > ALT:** Consider alcohol, muscle damage

### Kidney Function

**Creatinine**
| Creatinine | Interpretation |
|------------|----------------|
| 0.7-1.2 | Normal (varies by muscle mass) |
| 1.2-1.5 | Mild elevation — investigate |
| 1.5-3.0 | Moderate kidney impairment |
| >3.0 | Significant impairment — nephrology |

**BUN (Blood Urea Nitrogen)**
| BUN | Interpretation |
|-----|----------------|
| 7-16 | Optimal |
| <7 | Low protein intake, liver issues |
| 17-20 | Upper normal — check hydration |
| >20 | Dehydration, kidney issues, high protein, GI bleed |

**BUN/Creatinine ratio:**
- 10-16: Normal
- >20: Dehydration, GI bleed, high protein catabolism
- <10: Liver disease, low protein, rhabdomyolysis

**eGFR (Estimated Glomerular Filtration Rate)**
| eGFR (mL/min/1.73m²) | CKD Stage | Interpretation |
|----------------------|-----------|----------------|
| >90 | Normal | Normal kidney function |
| 60-89 | Stage 2 | Mildly decreased |
| 45-59 | Stage 3a | Mild-moderate decrease |
| 30-44 | Stage 3b | Moderate-severe decrease |
| 15-29 | Stage 4 | Severely decreased |
| <15 | Stage 5 | Kidney failure |

**Cystatin C**
More accurate than creatinine, especially in:
- Elderly patients
- Muscle wasting
- Vegetarians
- Extremes of body size
| Cystatin C | Interpretation |
|------------|----------------|
| 0.6-1.0 mg/L | Normal |
| >1.0 | Decreased kidney function |

**ACR (Urine Albumin-to-Creatinine Ratio)**
| ACR (mg/g) | Category | Clinical Significance |
|------------|----------|----------------------|
| <30 | Normal | Healthy kidneys |
| 30-300 | Moderately increased | Microalbuminuria — early damage |
| >300 | Severely increased | Macroalbuminuria — significant damage |

**Microalbuminuria significance:**
- Early marker of kidney damage (before creatinine rises)
- MUST check in: diabetes, hypertension, CV disease
- Indicates endothelial dysfunction (systemic, not just kidney)
- Predictor of cardiovascular events

**When to test kidney function:**
- Baseline in hypertension (ALWAYS)
- Diabetes (annual ACR minimum)
- Before starting nephrotoxic drugs (NSAIDs, ACE-I, ARB)
- Heart failure
- Family history of kidney disease
- Age >60

### Electrolytes

| Electrolyte | Optimal Range |
|-------------|---------------|
| Sodium | 140-142 |
| Potassium | 4.0-4.5 |
| Chloride | 100-106 |
| CO2 | 25-30 |

---

## LIPID PANEL

### Standard Lipids

| Marker | Lab "Normal" | Optimal | Notes |
|--------|--------------|---------|-------|
| Total Cholesterol | <200 | — | Limited utility alone |
| LDL-C | <100 | <100 | Particle number matters more |
| HDL-C | >40 M, >50 F | >60 | Higher generally better |
| Triglycerides | <150 | <100 | Metabolic marker |
| TG/HDL ratio | — | <2.0 | Insulin resistance proxy |

### Advanced Lipids (More Useful)

| Marker | Optimal | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| ApoB | <80 mg/dL | Best single lipid marker |
| LDL-P | <1000 nmol/L | Particle number |
| Lp(a) | <30 mg/dL | Genetic, test once |
| oxLDL | Low | Oxidized (dangerous) LDL |

---

## THYROID PANEL

| Marker | Lab Range | Optimal Range |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| TSH | 0.4-4.5 | 0.5-2.0 |
| Free T4 | 0.8-1.8 | 1.0-1.5 |
| Free T3 | 2.3-4.2 | 3.0-4.0 |
| Reverse T3 | 9-27 | <15 |
| TPO Antibodies | <35 | <35 (negative) |
| TG Antibodies | <35 | <35 (negative) |

**RT3/FT3 ratio:** Should be >0.2

---

## IRON STUDIES

| Marker | Optimal | Notes |
|--------|---------|-------|
| Ferritin | 50-150 (women), 75-150 (men) | Storage iron |
| Serum Iron | 60-170 | Variable |
| TIBC | 250-350 | Carrying capacity |
| % Saturation | 25-35% | Iron bound to transferrin |

**Ferritin interpretation:**
- <20: Deficient
- 20-50: Suboptimal
- 50-150: Optimal for most
- >200 (women), >300 (men): Investigate (iron overload, inflammation)

**Ferritin is also an acute phase reactant** — Elevated in inflammation.

---

## COMPLETE BLOOD COUNT

### Red Blood Cells

| Marker | Optimal | Low Suggests | High Suggests |
|--------|---------|--------------|---------------|
| RBC | 4.5-5.0 (M), 4.0-4.5 (F) | Anemia | Polycythemia |
| Hemoglobin | 14-16 (M), 12-14 (F) | Anemia | Dehydration, polycythemia |
| Hematocrit | 40-48 (M), 36-44 (F) | Anemia | Dehydration, TRT effect |
| MCV | 85-95 | B12/folate def (high), Iron def (low) | — |
| MCH | 28-32 | — | — |
| MCHC | 32-36 | — | — |
| RDW | 11.5-14.0 | — | Mixed anemia, chronic disease |

### White Blood Cells

| Marker | Optimal | Low Suggests | High Suggests |
|--------|---------|--------------|---------------|
| WBC | 5.0-7.5 | Immune suppression | Infection, inflammation |
| Neutrophils | 50-60% | — | Bacterial infection |
| Lymphocytes | 25-40% | Viral infection | — |
| Monocytes | 4-8% | — | Chronic infection |
| Eosinophils | 1-4% | — | Allergies, parasites |
| Basophils | <1% | — | Allergies |

### Platelets

| Range | Interpretation |
|-------|----------------|
| 150-400 | Normal |
| <150 | Thrombocytopenia (investigate) |
| >400 | Thrombocytosis (investigate) |

---

## INFLAMMATION MARKERS

| Marker | Optimal | Elevated Suggests |
|--------|---------|-------------------|
| hs-CRP | <1.0 mg/L | Inflammation (cardiac risk if >3) |
| ESR | <10 mm/hr | Inflammation (less specific) |
| Homocysteine | <8 µmol/L | Inflammation, methylation issues |
| Ferritin (elevated) | See above | Can indicate inflammation |

---

## HORMONE TESTING — OPTIMAL RANGES

### Male Hormones

| Marker | Lab Range | Optimal |
|--------|-----------|---------|
| Total Testosterone | 300-1000 | 600-900 ng/dL |
| Free Testosterone (direct/ED) | 5-21 | 15-25 pg/mL |
| Free Testosterone (calculated) | 35-155 | 100-250 pg/mL |
| SHBG | 10-57 | 20-40 nmol/L |
| Estradiol (sensitive) | 10-40 | 20-30 pg/mL |
| LH | 1.5-9.0 | Varies by situation |
| FSH | 1.5-12 | Varies by situation |

**Free Testosterone — CRITICAL: TWO ASSAYS, TWO SCALES**

Free T is reported in two fundamentally different ways depending on the lab:

**Direct / Equilibrium Dialysis (ED):** Measures actual unbound testosterone.
Range 5-21 pg/mL. Optimal 15-25 pg/mL. This is the reference standard.

**Calculated (Vermeulen equation):** Derived from Total T, SHBG, and albumin.
Range 35-155 pg/mL. Optimal 100-250 pg/mL. Used by OptimalDX, many functional
labs, and most standard panels that report "Free T" without specifying method.

**The same patient will show ~5x higher numbers on calculated vs direct.**
A calculated Free T of 95 pg/mL is LOW (37th percentile). A direct Free T
of 95 pg/mL would be extremely high (>99th percentile). Always verify which
assay the lab used before interpreting. If not specified, assume calculated.

**% Free Testosterone** is method-independent: optimal >2.0% for men.
This is the safest cross-lab comparison metric when assay method is unknown.

### Female Hormones

**Varies by cycle phase and menopausal status.**

| Marker | Follicular | Ovulation | Luteal | Menopause |
|--------|------------|-----------|--------|-----------|
| Estradiol | 30-100 | 100-400 | 50-200 | <30 |
| Progesterone | <1 | — | >10 ideal | <1 |
| FSH | 3-10 | >20 (surge) | 3-10 | >30 |
| LH | 2-10 | >20 (surge) | 2-10 | >30 |

### Both Genders

| Marker | Optimal |
|--------|---------|
| DHEA-S | Mid-upper range for age |
| Cortisol (AM) | 10-18 µg/dL |
| IGF-1 | 150-250 ng/mL (varies by age) |

**Cortisol AM — SINGLE SNAPSHOT LIMITATION**

A single fasting AM cortisol is a SCREENING tool, not a diagnostic tool.
It captures one point on a 24-hour rhythm that is highly variable.

| AM Cortisol | Interpretation | Next Step |
|-------------|---------------|-----------|
| >15 µg/dL | GREEN — adrenal terrain adequate | Proceed |
| 10-15 µg/dL | YELLOW — suboptimal, context-dependent | Repeat or 4-point salivary |
| <10 µg/dL | RED — suggestive of insufficiency | Confirm with DUTCH or 4-point salivary before classifying |
| <5 µg/dL | CRITICAL — adrenal crisis risk | Urgent endocrinology |

Confounders: late wake time, poor sleep night before, acute stress, recent
illness, medications (corticosteroids, opioids). A value of 8-10 µg/dL in
an otherwise asymptomatic patient may be a timing artifact. Always correlate
with DHEA-S, clinical presentation, and ideally a dynamic or diurnal test
before making protocol decisions.

**Do not gate or deny interventions based solely on a single AM cortisol
between 8-10 µg/dL.** Confirm the pattern first.

---

## VITAMIN AND MINERAL LEVELS

| Nutrient | Optimal | Deficient |
|----------|---------|-----------|
| Vitamin D (25-OH) | 50-80 ng/mL | <30 |
| Vitamin B12 | >500 pg/mL | <400 (functional def) |
| Folate | >15 ng/mL | <10 |
| Ferritin | 50-150 | <50 |
| RBC Magnesium | 5.5-6.5 mg/dL | <5.0 |
| Zinc (plasma) | 80-120 µg/dL | <70 |
| Copper | 70-155 µg/dL | — |
| Zinc/Copper ratio | 0.8-1.2 | — |
| Selenium | 110-150 µg/L | <110 |
| Omega-3 Index | >8% | <4% (high risk) |

---

 === SECTION 2: PATIENT COMMUNICATION [PATIENT_OK] ===
## COMPREHENSIVE LAB PANELS

### Foundation Panel

| Category | Tests |
|----------|-------|
| Metabolic | CMP (glucose, kidney, liver, electrolytes) |
| Complete Blood | CBC with differential |
| Lipids | Standard + TG/HDL ratio |
| Thyroid | TSH, Free T4, Free T3 |
| Inflammation | hs-CRP, homocysteine |
| Iron | Ferritin, iron, TIBC, % saturation |
| Vitamins | Vitamin D, B12, folate |

### Extended Panel

**Add to foundation:**

| Category | Tests |
|----------|-------|
| Advanced Lipids | ApoB, Lp(a), LDL-P (if available) |
| Metabolic depth | Fasting insulin, HbA1c, HOMA-IR |
| Full Thyroid | Reverse T3, TPO, TG antibodies |
| Hormones | Per gender (see 17/18) |
| Advanced | RBC magnesium, Omega-3 index, zinc |

### Condition-Specific Additions

| Condition | Additional Tests |
|-----------|------------------|
| Autoimmune | ANA, specific antibodies, ESR |
| Cardiovascular | ApoB, Lp(a), CAC score |
| Gut issues | GI-MAP, zonulin |
| Cognitive | Homocysteine, B vitamins, ApoE |
| Fatigue | Full thyroid, adrenal (DUTCH), ferritin, B12 |
| Inflammation | hs-CRP, ESR, fibrinogen |
| Mold/CIRS | C4a, TGF-β1, MMP-9, MSH, VIP |

## PATTERN RECOGNITION

### Metabolic Syndrome Pattern

| Marker | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Fasting glucose | >100 |
| Fasting insulin | >10 |
| Triglycerides | >150 |
| HDL | <40 M, <50 F |
| HOMA-IR | >2.0 |
| hs-CRP | Often elevated |
| Uric acid | Often elevated |
| Liver enzymes | May be elevated (fatty liver) |

### Hypothyroid Pattern

| Marker | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| TSH | >2.5 (or may be "normal" range) |
| Free T4 | Lower half or below |
| Free T3 | Low or lower half |
| Reverse T3 | May be elevated |
| TPO antibodies | May be positive (Hashimoto's) |
| Cholesterol | Often elevated |
| Ferritin | May be low |

### Adrenal Dysfunction Pattern

| Marker | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| AM Cortisol | May be high, low, or "normal" |
| DHEA-S | Often low |
| Cortisol pattern (DUTCH) | Flat, inverted, or erratic |
| Sodium | May be low-normal |
| Potassium | May be high-normal |
| Glucose | May be unstable |
| Thyroid | May appear low (cortisol affects conversion) |

### Iron Deficiency Pattern

| Marker | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| Ferritin | <50 (or <20 = definite deficiency) |
| Serum Iron | Low |
| TIBC | High (body trying to get more iron) |
| % Saturation | Low |
| RBC/Hemoglobin | May be low (late finding) |
| MCV | Low (microcytic) |
| RDW | Elevated |

### B12 Deficiency Pattern

| Marker | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| B12 | <400 (or even <600 can be functional) |
| MCV | High (macrocytic) |
| Homocysteine | Elevated |
| MMA | Elevated (more specific) |
| Neurological symptoms | May precede lab changes |

### Inflammatory Pattern

| Marker | Finding |
|--------|---------|
| hs-CRP | >1.0 |
| ESR | Elevated |
| Ferritin | May be elevated |
| WBC | May be elevated |
| Platelets | May be elevated |
| Albumin | May be low |
| Homocysteine | Often elevated |


## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
## CALCULATED RATIOS & DERIVED INDICES
## ═══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Valentine MUST calculate and interpret these ratios whenever the raw values
are available in a patient panel. These derived indices reveal cross-system
dysfunction that individual markers miss. Each ratio links to a coupled
system file for mechanistic depth.

**7-TIER SCORING SYSTEM:**
All ratios below use a standardized 7-tier scale:
VERY LOW → LOW → MODERATELY LOW → OPTIMAL → MODERATELY HIGH → HIGH → VERY HIGH
This mirrors how individual biomarkers are scored and ensures consistent
language across the entire dossier.

---

### METABOLIC INDICES

#### TyG Index (Triglyceride-Glucose Index)
**Formula:** ln[Triglycerides (mg/dL) × Fasting Glucose (mg/dL) / 2]
**Coupled system:** 06_METABOLIC

**What it tells you:** Surrogate marker for insulin resistance that does NOT
require fasting insulin (which many labs omit). More robust than glucose or
TG alone. Validated against hyperinsulinemic-euglycemic clamp.

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <4.5 | Excellent insulin sensitivity |
| Moderately High | 4.5-4.7 | Early metabolic strain — lifestyle focus |
| High | 4.71-5.0 | Insulin resistance developing — order fasting insulin + HOMA-IR |
| Very High | >5.0 | Significant insulin resistance — active metabolic intervention |

**Clinical decision:** TyG <4.5 with FBG 95-100 = likely cortisol dawn effect
(check AM cortisol). TyG >4.7 with FBG 95-100 = true metabolic strain.
This distinction matters: Patrick Joore had TyG 4.34 + FBG 90 → cortisol
dawn effect, NOT prediabetes. Without TyG, FBG alone would mislead.

#### Estimated Average Glucose (eAG)
**Formula:** eAG (mg/dL) = (28.7 × HbA1c) − 46.7
**Coupled system:** 06_METABOLIC

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | 90-105 mg/dL | HbA1c <5.3% equivalent |
| Moderately High | 105-125 mg/dL | HbA1c 5.3-6.0% — early metabolic drift |
| High | 125-154 mg/dL | HbA1c 6.0-7.0% — prediabetes/diabetes territory |
| Very High | >154 mg/dL | HbA1c >7.0% — uncontrolled |

**Note:** eAG is a patient communication tool — translates HbA1c into a number
patients recognize. "Your average sugar over 3 months was 111" is more
intuitive than "Your HbA1c is 5.5%."

#### C-Peptide (Insulin Production Proxy)
**Coupled system:** 06_METABOLIC

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.4 ng/mL | Insufficient insulin production — evaluate type 1 / LADA |
| Low | 0.4-0.69 ng/mL | Reduced production — further workup |
| Moderately Low | 0.7-0.79 ng/mL | Borderline — monitor |
| Optimal | 0.8-2.1 ng/mL | Normal insulin production and sensitivity |
| Moderately High | 2.11-3.0 ng/mL | Early overproduction — insulin resistance developing |
| High | 3.01-4.0 ng/mL | Significant overproduction — active metabolic intervention |
| Very High | >4.0 ng/mL | Advanced insulin resistance |

**Why it matters:** C-peptide is more stable than insulin in blood (longer
half-life, not affected by exogenous insulin). When fasting insulin is
unavailable, C-peptide + glucose can estimate metabolic health. Low C-peptide
+ high glucose = production failure (Type 1/LADA). High C-peptide + high
glucose = resistance (Type 2).

---

### CARDIOVASCULAR RATIOS

#### TC/HDL Ratio (Total Cholesterol / HDL)
**Formula:** Total Cholesterol ÷ HDL-C
**Coupled system:** 19_CARDIOVASCULAR

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <3.5 | Low cardiovascular risk |
| Moderately High | 3.5-4.5 | Acceptable but improvable |
| High | 4.51-5.5 | Elevated risk — investigate lipid drivers |
| Very High | >5.5 | Significant risk — ApoB + Lp(a) mandatory |

**Clinical decision:** TC/HDL >4.5 with TG <100 = clearance problem (thyroid?
See 57_THYROID_COMPLETE). TC/HDL >4.5 with TG >150 = metabolic driver.

#### LDL:HDL Ratio
**Formula:** LDL-C ÷ HDL-C
**Coupled system:** 19_CARDIOVASCULAR

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <2.3 | Favorable balance |
| Moderately High | 2.3-3.0 | Watch — check ApoB |
| High | 3.01-3.5 | Elevated atherogenic pressure |
| Very High | >3.5 | Significant — ApoB + Lp(a) + intervention planning |

#### Atherogenic Index of Plasma (AIP)
**Formula:** log10(Triglycerides / HDL-C) — both in mg/dL
**Coupled system:** 19_CARDIOVASCULAR

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <0.1 | Low risk — favorable large buoyant LDL profile |
| Moderately High | 0.1-0.24 | Intermediate risk |
| High | >0.24 | High risk — small dense LDL particles predominate |

**Why it matters:** AIP predicts LDL particle SIZE. Negative AIP (like Patrick
Joore's -0.17) means large buoyant LDL predominates — less atherogenic even
if LDL-C is elevated. Positive AIP + elevated LDL-C = urgent ApoB check.

#### Lipoprotein(a) — Lp(a)
**Coupled system:** 19_CARDIOVASCULAR

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <30 nmol/L (or <14 mg/dL) | Low genetic risk |
| Moderately High | 30-50 nmol/L | Moderate — lifestyle + monitor |
| High | 50.1-75 nmol/L | Elevated genetic burden — comprehensive CV strategy |
| Very High | >75 nmol/L (or >30 mg/dL) | High genetic risk — aggressive preventive approach |

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- Genetic — test ONCE in lifetime, does not change with lifestyle
- NOT affected by statins, diet, exercise, or most supplements
- Lp(a) OVERRIDES all other lipid calculations for risk stratification
- High Lp(a) + high ApoB = highest-risk combination
- Report in nmol/L preferred (mg/dL conversion is non-linear, unreliable)
- See 19_CARDIOVASCULAR for full Lp(a) management framework

#### ApoB (Apolipoprotein B)
**Coupled system:** 19_CARDIOVASCULAR

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <60 mg/dL | Lowest atherogenic particle burden |
| Moderately High | 60-80 mg/dL | Acceptable for low-risk, investigate if other CV flags |
| High | 80.1-100 mg/dL | Elevated particle burden — active management |
| Very High | >100 mg/dL | High particle count — aggressive intervention warranted |

**CRITICAL RULES:**
- ApoB = single best lipid marker. One ApoB molecule per atherogenic particle.
- ApoB >90 with LDL <130 = LDL-C UNDERESTIMATES risk (discordance)
- ApoB <70 with LDL >150 = LDL-C OVERESTIMATES risk (discordance)
- NEVER finalize a cardiovascular intervention plan without ApoB
- When ApoB and LDL-C disagree, ApoB wins

---

### INFLAMMATION & IMMUNE INDICES

#### NLR (Neutrophil-to-Lymphocyte Ratio)
**Formula:** Absolute Neutrophils ÷ Absolute Lymphocytes
**Coupled system:** 11_IMMUNE, 19_CARDIOVASCULAR

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.5 | Immunosuppression — bone marrow or chronic viral |
| Low | 0.5-0.79 | Low — monitor |
| Moderately Low | 0.8-0.99 | Borderline low |
| Optimal | 1.0-1.7 | Balanced immune function |
| Moderately High | 1.71-3.0 | Subclinical inflammation — investigate source |
| High | 3.01-4.5 | Chronic inflammation — active infection, stress, or disease |
| Very High | >4.5 | Significant systemic inflammation — urgent evaluation |

**Clinical decision:**
- NLR >3 in otherwise healthy patient → check hsCRP, rule out chronic infection
- NLR >3 + hsCRP >3 = confirmed inflammatory state → gate intensive protocols
- NLR elevated post-exercise (24-48h) is NORMAL — recheck after 72h rest
- NLR is a prognostic marker in cardiovascular disease, cancer, and metabolic syndrome

#### PLR (Platelet-to-Lymphocyte Ratio)
**Formula:** Platelets ÷ Absolute Lymphocytes
**Coupled system:** 11_IMMUNE

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | 60-128 | Balanced |
| Moderately High | 128.1-150 | Mild stress load — correlate with NLR |
| High | 150.1-262 | Elevated systemic stress — chronic inflammation or immune activation |
| Very High | >262 | Significant — evaluate for chronic disease, autoimmune, malignancy |

**Clinical decision:** PLR + NLR together are more predictive than either alone.
Both elevated = systemic inflammatory burden. PLR elevated but NLR normal =
platelet-driven (check MPV — high MPV + high PLR = thrombotic risk).

#### MPV (Mean Platelet Volume)
**Coupled system:** 19_CARDIOVASCULAR, 11_IMMUNE

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <6.9 fL | Bone marrow dysfunction — investigate |
| Low | 7.0-7.49 fL | Low-normal |
| Optimal | 7.5-8.2 fL | Normal platelet production |
| Moderately High | 8.21-11.5 fL | Increased platelet activity — correlate with platelets |
| High | 11.51-13.0 fL | Elevated — independent CV risk factor |
| Very High | >13.0 fL | Significant platelet activation |

**CRITICAL:** High MPV + high platelet count = high inflammation + thrombotic
risk. High MPV + LOW platelet count = compensatory production (consumption
coagulopathy, immune thrombocytopenia). Different clinical meaning entirely.

#### Homocysteine (Methylation & Vascular Marker)
**Coupled system:** 19_CARDIOVASCULAR, 03_GENETIC

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <7.2 umol/L | Good methylation, low vascular risk |
| Moderately High | 7.21-10 umol/L | Suboptimal methylation — B-vitamin optimization |
| High | 10.01-15 umol/L | Elevated — active B6/B12/folate intervention |
| Very High | >15 umol/L | Significantly elevated — investigate MTHFR, B12 deficit, renal |

---

### LIVER INDICES

#### AST:ALT Ratio
**Formula:** AST ÷ ALT
**Coupled system:** 13_DETOX

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <0.4 | Rare — isolated ALT elevation, consider medication effect |
| Low | 0.4-0.59 | ALT predominant — suggests NAFLD or metabolic liver strain |
| Moderately Low | 0.6-0.79 | Mild ALT predominance |
| Optimal | 0.8-1.0 | Balanced hepatic function |
| Moderately High | 1.01-1.5 | AST predominant — consider muscle damage (check CK), or alcohol |
| High | 1.51-2.0 | AST predominant — alcoholic liver disease or advanced fibrosis likely |
| Very High | >2.0 | Strongly suggests alcoholic hepatitis or cirrhosis — specialist referral |

**Clinical decision:**
- AST:ALT <0.8 + BMI >30 = NAFLD pattern — check liver ultrasound
- AST:ALT >1.0 + CK elevated = likely MUSCLE contribution, not liver
- AST:ALT >2.0 + GGT elevated = alcoholic liver disease pattern
- Post-exercise: AST rises from muscle (found in both liver + muscle).
  ALWAYS check CK. If CK elevated + AST:ALT >1.0, subtract muscle contribution.
  ALT is more liver-specific.

#### GGT — Functional Interpretation (Expanded)

Standard interpretation: liver/toxin marker. But GGT has a DUAL role:

**Too HIGH (>30 IU/L):** Toxin exposure, alcohol, fatty liver, medication load,
oxidative stress. Conventional interpretation applies.

**Too LOW (<10 IU/L):** GGT requires B6 (pyridoxal phosphate) and magnesium as
enzymatic cofactors. Very low GGT is a SIGNAL of B6 + Mg depletion — not a
sign of superior liver health.

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5 IU/L | Severe cofactor depletion — B6 + Mg likely deficient |
| Low | 5-7.9 IU/L | Cofactor insufficiency probable |
| Moderately Low | 8-9.9 IU/L | Borderline — assess dietary B6/Mg status |
| Optimal | 10-17 IU/L | Healthy enzymatic function |
| Moderately High | 17.1-30 IU/L | Mild elevation — toxin exposure, medication, alcohol |
| High | 30.1-50 IU/L | Significant — liver stress, fatty liver, or environmental toxins |
| Very High | >50 IU/L | Investigate: alcohol, NAFLD, hepatobiliary disease |

**Example:** Patrick Joore GGT 9.0 → "too low" is actually a B6 + Mg depletion
signal. Combined with MCHC 33.6 (low) and RDW 12.8 (borderline high), this
points to micronutrient insufficiency pattern, not healthy liver.

---

### KIDNEY INDICES

#### eGFR — Functional 7-Tier Scale

Standard CKD staging is useful for nephrology triage but misses the
functional optimization window. This 7-tier scale adds granularity:

| Tier | Range (mL/min/1.73m2) | Interpretation |
|------|----------------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <40 | CKD Stage 3b-5 — nephrology referral |
| Low | 40-79.9 | Mildly-moderately reduced — investigate cause |
| Moderately Low | 80-89.9 | Functional suboptimal — age-dependent, check hydration + creatinine source |
| Optimal | 90-120 | Normal kidney function |
| Moderately High | 120.1-160 | Hyperfiltration — may indicate early diabetes, high cardiac output |
| High | 160.1-180 | Significant hyperfiltration — evaluate |
| Very High | >180 | Pathological hyperfiltration |

**Age-adjustment caveat:** eGFR 76-85 in a 55+ patient may be age-expected,
especially if creatinine is elevated from muscle mass (athletic male). Check
cystatin C for confirmation. Do NOT alarm patient with "low kidney function"
when creatinine is 1.2 in a muscular 57-year-old male.

#### Uric Acid
**Coupled system:** 06_METABOLIC, 20_JOINTS_MSK

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <2.5 mg/dL | Rare — liver dysfunction, malnutrition |
| Low | 2.5-3.44 mg/dL | Low normal |
| Moderately Low | 3.45-3.49 mg/dL | Borderline |
| Optimal | 3.5-5.4 mg/dL | Normal purine metabolism |
| Moderately High | 5.41-8.0 mg/dL | Elevated — gout risk rising, insulin resistance association |
| High | 8.01-11.0 mg/dL | High — active management, target <6 mg/dL |
| Very High | >11.0 mg/dL | Significant — gout, kidney stones, metabolic syndrome, nephrology |

**Clinical decision:** Uric acid >6 mg/dL + insulin resistance markers elevated
= metabolic syndrome contributor. Uric acid is BOTH a marker and a CAUSE of
endothelial dysfunction — it is not just about gout.

---

### MINERAL & HYDRATION INDICES

#### Ca:P Ratio (Calcium : Phosphorus)
**Formula:** Serum Calcium / Serum Phosphorus
**Coupled system:** 20_JOINTS_MSK (bone/mineral metabolism)

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <1.5 | Severe imbalance — excess phosphorus or critically low calcium |
| Low | 1.51-1.9 | Imbalanced — evaluate PTH, Vitamin D, kidney function |
| Moderately Low | 2.0-2.29 | Borderline — dietary assessment |
| Optimal | 2.3-3.2 | Balanced mineral metabolism |
| Moderately High | 3.21-3.5 | Mild calcium excess or low phosphorus |
| High | 3.51-4.0 | Evaluate: hypercalcemia, Vitamin D excess, low phosphorus absorption |
| Very High | >4.0 | Investigate PTH, Vitamin D toxicity, malignancy |

**Why it matters:** Ratio >3.5 with Vitamin D >100 ng/mL → Vitamin D excess
pushing calcium up. Ratio <2.0 → excess phosphorus (processed food diet) or
calcium malabsorption. Both affect bone mineralization and soft tissue
calcification risk.

#### Na:K Index (Sodium : Potassium)
**Formula:** Serum Sodium / Serum Potassium
**Coupled system:** 09_HPA_ADRENAL, 39_HYDRATION_ELECTROLYTES

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <26 | Low — hyponatremia pattern, adrenal insufficiency? |
| Low | 26-28.99 | Low normal — check adrenal, hydration |
| Moderately Low | 29-29.99 | Borderline |
| Optimal | 30-35 | Balanced adrenal and fluid regulation |
| Moderately High | 35.1-36 | Mild elevation — stress, dehydration |
| High | 36.1-38 | Elevated — chronic stress pattern, hyperaldosteronism? |
| Very High | >38 | Investigate adrenal, kidney, dehydration |

**Clinical decision:** Na:K >36 + Cortisol AM elevated = chronic stress pattern.
Na:K <29 + Cortisol AM <10 = adrenal insufficiency pattern. Correlate with
09_HPA_ADRENAL before interpreting.

#### Osmolality Index (Calculated — Fasted)
**Formula:** 2 x Na + (BUN / 2.8) + (Glucose / 18)
**Coupled system:** 39_HYDRATION_ELECTROLYTES

| Tier | Range (mOsm/kg) | Interpretation |
|------|-----------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <275 | Overhydrated or dilutional — hyponatremia? |
| Low | 275-284.9 | Low — possible overhydration |
| Moderately Low | 285-286.9 | Borderline low |
| Optimal | 287-291 | Euhydrated |
| Moderately High | 291.1-295 | Concentrated — suboptimal hydration relative to demand |
| High | 295.1-300 | Dehydrated — especially significant in athletes |
| Very High | >300 | Significant dehydration — evaluate kidney, diabetes insipidus |

**Clinical decision in athletes:** Osmolality >291 in a regular trainer with
intense sessions = chronic underhydration. Combined with low Cl (<100),
low Mg, high Na → electrolyte strategy needed, not just "drink more water."
Daniele B pattern: Osmolality 295 + Cl 94.9 + Mg 1.94 = textbook
intracellular dehydration despite adequate water intake.

#### Chloride — Functional Scale

| Tier | Range (mEq/L) | Interpretation |
|------|---------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <90 | Significant — metabolic alkalosis, vomiting, adrenal? |
| Low | 90-94.9 | Electrolyte imbalance — often paired with dehydration pattern |
| Moderately Low | 95-99.9 | Borderline — assess full electrolyte picture |
| Optimal | 100-106 | Normal acid-base and fluid balance |
| Moderately High | 106.1-110 | Mildly elevated — check kidney, hydration |
| High | 110.1-115 | Elevated — metabolic acidosis? dehydration? |
| Very High | >115 | Significant — urgent electrolyte evaluation |

---

### HORMONAL RATIOS

#### Cortisol:DHEA Ratio
**Formula:** Cortisol AM (ug/dL) / DHEA-S (ug/dL)
**Coupled system:** 09_HPA_ADRENAL

Note: This uses the simplest clinical ratio. Cortisol in ug/dL divided by
DHEA-S in ug/dL gives a small decimal.

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <0.08 | Balanced stress resilience — healthy aging ratio |
| Moderately High | 0.08-0.14 | Mild stress predominance — cortisol winning vs DHEA |
| High | 0.15-0.2 | Chronic stress — DHEA depleted relative to cortisol |
| Very High | >0.2 | Significant stress imbalance — accelerated aging pattern |

**Clinical decision:** Ratio >0.15 = cortisol dominance. Even if BOTH are low,
the ratio matters — tells you which axis is failing faster. Ratio <0.05 with
both values adequate = excellent resilience marker.

#### Testosterone : Estradiol Ratio (Male)
**Formula:** Total Testosterone (ng/dL) / Estradiol (pg/mL)
**Coupled system:** 17_MALE_HORMONES

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5 | Severe estrogen dominance — investigate aromatase excess, liver |
| Low | 5-9.9 | Estrogen predominant — body fat? liver clearance? medication? |
| Moderately Low | 10-19.9 | Suboptimal balance |
| Optimal | 20-40 | Healthy androgen-estrogen balance |
| Moderately High | 40.1-60 | Testosterone predominant — acceptable if estradiol adequate |
| High | 60.1-80 | Consider: is estradiol too low? (E2 <20 in men is problematic) |
| Very High | >80 | Estradiol likely suppressed — bone, mood, CV risk from low E2 |

**CRITICAL:** A very high ratio is NOT always good. E2 <15 pg/mL in men →
bone loss, mood issues, cardiovascular risk. The ratio should be optimal
because BOTH values are in range, not because one is suppressed.

#### SHBG — Male Functional Scale (Expanded)

| Tier | Range (nmol/L) | Interpretation |
|------|----------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <5 | Investigate: liver disease, obesity, exogenous androgens |
| Low | 5-9.9 | Low — insulin resistance association, metabolic syndrome |
| Moderately Low | 10-39.9 | Below optimal — more free T available but possibly metabolic driver |
| Optimal | 40-46 | Balanced binding and availability |
| Moderately High | 46.1-50 | Mild elevation — beginning to sequester free T |
| High | 50.1-70 | Elevated — significant free T sequestration. NOT a TRT case unless free T confirmed low |
| Very High | >70 | Strongly elevated — liver? thyroid? evaluate before hormonal intervention |

**CRITICAL RULE:** High SHBG + low Free T is NOT automatic TRT. This is an SHBG
case. Fix SHBG drivers first (thyroid, cortisol, insulin, liver). If Free T
remains low after SHBG addressed → then evaluate TRT. See 17_MALE_HORMONES.

---

### HEMATOLOGY RATIOS

#### RDW (Red Cell Distribution Width) — Functional Scale

| Tier | Range | Interpretation |
|------|-------|----------------|
| Optimal | <12.6% | Uniform RBC production — good nutritional status |
| Moderately High | 12.61-14.0% | Mild variation — early nutritional deficit (B12? folate? iron?) |
| High | 14.1-16.0% | Significant variation — active investigation: mixed anemia, chronic disease |
| Very High | >16.0% | Wide variation — multiple deficiencies, chronic disease, or treatment response |

**Clinical decision:** RDW elevated + MCV high = B12/folate deficiency.
RDW elevated + MCV low = iron deficiency. RDW elevated + MCV normal =
mixed deficiency OR early deficiency (RDW changes before MCV does).
RDW is an EARLY warning marker — it often flags before anemia develops.

#### CPK (Creatine Phosphokinase) — Context-Dependent

| Tier | Range (U/L) | Interpretation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Very Low | <30 | Possible muscle wasting, inactivity, or sarcopenia |
| Low | 30-49.9 | Low muscle mass or low activity |
| Moderately Low | 50-64.9 | Borderline |
| Optimal | 65-196 | Normal muscle turnover |
| Moderately High | 196.1-250 | Mild elevation — post-exercise (24-72h) is NORMAL |
| High | 250.1-500 | Elevated — correlate with timing of exercise. >72h post-training = investigate |
| Very High | >500 | Significant — rhabdomyolysis risk if >5x upper limit. Hydrate + monitor kidney |

**SPORT CONTEXT (see 63_SPORT_PERFORMANCE_CONTEXT):**
CPK 200-500 post-heavy training (24-48h) = EXPECTED. Do not alarm.
CPK >1000 without recent extreme exercise = pathological — investigate.
Always ask: "When did you last train intensely?" before interpreting CPK.

---

### CROSS-SYSTEM PATTERN RECOGNITION — RATIO COMBINATIONS

These ratio COMBINATIONS reveal cross-system dysfunction that individual
ratios miss. Valentine should check these patterns whenever multiple ratios
are available:

#### Metabolic-Inflammatory Pattern
NLR >2.5 + TyG >4.7 + hsCRP >1.0 → Metabolic inflammation — insulin
resistance driving immune activation. Priority: fix metabolic terrain FIRST.
Coupled: 06_METABOLIC + 11_IMMUNE

#### Hormonal-Thyroid-Clearance Pattern
TC/HDL >4.5 + TG/HDL <2.0 + SHBG >50 + Free T low → Thyroid clearance
problem driving lipids AND hormone sequestration. NOT a statin case. NOT a
TRT case. Fix thyroid, expect both axes to improve.
Coupled: 57_THYROID_COMPLETE + 19_CARDIOVASCULAR + 17_MALE_HORMONES

#### Stress-Depletion Pattern
Cortisol:DHEA >0.15 + Na:K >35 + GGT <10 + Mg low → Chronic stress
depleting mineral and B-vitamin cofactors. The stress axis is consuming
resources faster than supply. Priority: adrenal support + micronutrient
repletion before any intensive protocol.
Coupled: 09_HPA_ADRENAL + 39_HYDRATION_ELECTROLYTES

#### Intracellular Dehydration Pattern
Osmolality >291 + Cl <100 + Mg <2.0 + Na moderately high → Blood is
concentrated but cells are dry. "Drinking water" alone won't fix it —
electrolyte strategy with Mg, K, and proper Na balance required.
Coupled: 39_HYDRATION_ELECTROLYTES

#### Aromatization Pattern (Male)
T:E2 <15 + SHBG low + body fat >25% + estradiol >40 pg/mL →
Testosterone being converted to estrogen via aromatase in adipose tissue.
Address body composition and liver estrogen clearance before hormonal
intervention. DIM, cruciferous, zinc, weight loss.
Coupled: 17_MALE_HORMONES + 29_WEIGHT_BODY_COMP

#### Occult Anemia Pattern
MCHC <33 + RDW >13% + GGT <10 + Total Protein low → Red cells
under-packed, size variation increasing, B-vitamin cofactors depleted,
protein substrate insufficient. The anemia is developing BEFORE hemoglobin
drops. Act now: B-complex, iron status check, protein optimization.
Coupled: 04_NUTRITION_DIET + 06_METABOLIC

---

## DUTCH TEST INTERPRETATION

### Cortisol Patterns

| Pattern | Finding | Suggests |
|---------|---------|----------|
| High total, normal rhythm | Elevated cortisol morning, drops appropriately | Stress response, early HPA activation |
| High flat | Elevated but minimal change | Chronic stress, HPA dysfunction |
| Low total, normal rhythm | Low but appropriate pattern | "Adrenal fatigue," late HPA |
| Low flat | Low and no variation | Significant HPA dysfunction |
| Inverted | Low AM, high PM | Circadian disruption |

### Estrogen Metabolism

| Pathway | Optimal | High Suggests |
|---------|---------|---------------|
| 2-OH | 60-70% of metabolism | Protective |
| 4-OH | <10% | DNA damage, cancer risk |
| 16-OH | 20-30% | Proliferative |

**Support 2-OH pathway:** DIM, cruciferous vegetables
**If high 4-OH:** Support methylation (methylation clears 4-OH)

### Androgen Metabolism

| Marker | Notes |
|--------|-------|
| High 5α-reductase activity | More DHT, may see acne, hair loss |
| Low androgens overall | Fatigue, low libido, depression |
| High androgens (women) | PCOS pattern, acne, hirsutism |

## GI-MAP INTERPRETATION

### Key Markers

| Category | Marker | High Suggests |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Pathogens | H. pylori + virulence factors | Treat if symptomatic |
| Pathogens | Parasites detected | Consider treatment |
| Inflammation | Calprotectin >50 | Intestinal inflammation |
| Permeability | Zonulin elevated | Leaky gut |
| Immunity | sIgA low | Poor mucosal defense |
| Immunity | sIgA high | Acute infection/inflammation |
| Digestion | Elastase low | Pancreatic insufficiency |
| Digestion | Steatocrit high | Fat malabsorption |
| Metabolism | Beta-glucuronidase high | Estrogen recirculation |

---

 === SECTION 3: PHARMACOKINETICS & DYNAMICS (ADME) [INTERNAL_ONLY] ===
## OPTIMAL VS. LAB NORMAL

| Test | Lab "Normal" | Optimal |
|------|--------------|---------|
| Fasting glucose | 65-99 | 70-85 |
| Fasting insulin | 2.6-24.9 | <5 |
| HbA1c | <5.7% | <5.3% |
| TSH | 0.4-4.5 | 0.5-2.0 |
| Free T3 | 2.3-4.2 | 3.0-4.0 |
| Vitamin D | 30-100 | 50-80 |
| B12 | 200-900 | >500 |
| Ferritin | 12-150 (F), 12-300 (M) | 50-150 |
| hs-CRP | <3.0 | <1.0 |
| Homocysteine | 5-15 | <8 |
| Total T (men) | 300-1000 | 600-900 |

## KEY RATIOS — COMPLETE REFERENCE

### Metabolic
| Ratio | Formula | Optimal | Coupled System |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------|
| TG/HDL | TG ÷ HDL | <2.0 | 06_METABOLIC |
| TyG Index | ln(TG × FBG / 2) | <4.5 | 06_METABOLIC |
| HOMA-IR | (FBG × Insulin) ÷ 405 | <1.0 | 06_METABOLIC |
| eAG | (28.7 × HbA1c) − 46.7 | 90-105 mg/dL | 06_METABOLIC |

### Cardiovascular
| Ratio | Formula | Optimal | Coupled System |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------|
| TC/HDL | TC ÷ HDL | <3.5 | 19_CARDIOVASCULAR |
| LDL:HDL | LDL ÷ HDL | <2.3 | 19_CARDIOVASCULAR |
| AIP | log10(TG / HDL) | <0.1 | 19_CARDIOVASCULAR |
| BUN/Creatinine | BUN ÷ Cr | 10-16 | kidney |

### Inflammation & Immune
| Ratio | Formula | Optimal | Coupled System |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------|
| NLR | Neutro ÷ Lympho | 1.0-1.7 | 11_IMMUNE |
| PLR | Platelets ÷ Lympho | 60-128 | 11_IMMUNE |

### Liver
| Ratio | Formula | Optimal | Coupled System |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------|
| AST:ALT | AST ÷ ALT | 0.8-1.0 | 13_DETOX |

### Mineral & Hydration
| Ratio | Formula | Optimal | Coupled System |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------|
| Ca:P | Ca ÷ P | 2.3-3.2 | 20_JOINTS_MSK |
| Na:K | Na ÷ K | 30-35 | 09_HPA, 39_HYDRATION |
| Osmolality | 2×Na + BUN/2.8 + Glu/18 | 287-291 | 39_HYDRATION |
| Zinc/Copper | Zn ÷ Cu | 0.8-1.2 | mineral balance |

### Hormonal
| Ratio | Formula | Optimal | Coupled System |
|-------|---------|---------|----------------|
| Cortisol:DHEA | Cortisol ÷ DHEA-S | <0.08 | 09_HPA_ADRENAL |
| T:E2 (male) | Total T ÷ E2 | 20-40 | 17_MALE_HORMONES |
| % Free T | (Free T ÷ Total T) × 100 | >2.0% | 17_MALE_HORMONES |
| RT3/FT3 | rT3 ÷ FT3 | >0.2 | 57_THYROID |

## PATTERN QUICK GUIDE

| Pattern | Key Findings |
|---------|--------------|
| Metabolic syndrome | High glucose/insulin/TG, low HDL, TyG >4.7 |
| Hypothyroid | TSH >2.5, low FT3, high RT3, LDL elevated with TG optimal |
| Thyroid-lipid clearance | TC/HDL >4.5 + TG/HDL <2.0 + SHBG high |
| Iron deficiency | Low ferritin, low sat, high TIBC, low MCHC |
| B12 deficiency | Low B12, high MCV, high homocysteine |
| Occult anemia | MCHC <33, RDW >13%, GGT <10, low protein |
| Inflammation | High hs-CRP, ESR, ferritin, NLR >3 |
| HPA dysfunction | Low DHEA-S, abnormal cortisol pattern, Cortisol:DHEA >0.15 |
| Stress-depletion | Cortisol:DHEA >0.15, Na:K >35, GGT <10, Mg low |
| Intracellular dehydration | Osmolality >291, Cl <100, Mg <2.0, Na high-normal |
| Aromatization (male) | T:E2 <15, SHBG low, body fat >25%, E2 >40 |
| SHBG sequestration | SHBG >50, Free T low, % Free T <2.0%, Total T adequate |

## MUST-ORDER TESTS

**For comprehensive assessment:**
1. Fasting insulin (not just glucose)
2. Full thyroid panel (not just TSH)
3. Ferritin (not just hemoglobin)
4. Vitamin D
5. hs-CRP
6. Homocysteine

---
